title: Queues, Streams, Super Streams, Retention, and Replay · Contract Lens: Queue Stream Semantics
summary: A high-throughput replay workload uses one quorum queue with a ten-million-message backlog, while another service selects streams but expects destructive consumption and ordinary queue client locality. Planned artifact: Add and verify the queue stream semantics increment in queue-versus-stream workload decision and replay plan. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Compare destructive queue consumption with retained stream offsets, replay, retention, protocol clients, and consumer responsibility.
Measure throughput, tail latency, skew, recovery, and correctness when selecting queue count, consumer concurrency, and order scope.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: RabbitMQ and TypeScript Predict Queue Stream Semantics · Theory Queue Stream Semantics
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory messaging team handles case 4036 in a multi-tenant case event backbone with three subscriber responsibilities. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to choose queue, stream, or super stream from replay, backlog, throughput, and order-key evidence; keep credentials and connection URLs out of learner output; then add one subscriber responsibility without changing publisher intent. Browser code models pure messaging decisions only. amqplib, AMQP sockets, RabbitMQ, TLS, partitions, restarts, Docker, databases, external effects, load, upgrades, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates.
